title: Take a Random Walk Around the Berlin Wall Just Months Before Its Sudden Fall (Summer 1989)
description:
in History, Travel |   	May 27th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

OffiôÙcialôÙly, the Berlin Wall fell on NovemôÙber 9, 1989. DemoôÙliôÙtion would take m
body:
in History, Travel |   	May 27th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

OffiôÙcialôÙly, the Berlin Wall fell on NovemôÙber 9, 1989. DemoôÙliôÙtion would take more than four years, and a few secôÙtions remain for memoôÙrôÙiôÙal purôÙposôÙes, but it was on that date that pasôÙsage between East and West Berlin ã and thus East and West GerôÙmany ã opened to all citôÙiôÙzens of both counôÙtries. To say that it came as a surôÙprise would be a seriôÙous underôÙstateôÙment. EarôÙliôÙer that year, even the best informed observers were preôÙdictôÙing that the wall would stand for at least a few more decades. EarôÙliôÙer that day, for that matôÙter, the offiôÙcials involved in the openôÙing didôÙnãt foreôÙsee that SocialôÙist UniôÙty ParôÙty of GerôÙmany SecôÙreôÙtary of InforôÙmaôÙtion Gû¥nôÙter SchôÙabowsôÙki would, that evening, misôÙtakôÙenôÙly declare on nationôÙal teleôÙviôÙsion that the libôÙerôÙalôÙizaôÙtion of borôÙder travôÙel was effecôÙtive ãimmeôÙdiôÙateôÙly, withôÙout delay.ã
When the borôÙder guards finalôÙly gave up their attempts to hold the line around 11:00 that night, the surôÙroundôÙing scene in both Berlins had turned into what attenôÙdees now rememôÙber, 36 years latôÙer, as the biggest street fesôÙtiôÙval of their lives. To those of us unable to join in the celôÙeôÙbraôÙtion at the time, it may seem unlikeôÙly that such an event could realôÙly have occurred with no intiôÙmaôÙtions whatôÙsoôÙevôÙer.
Yet the footage shot by a travôÙelôÙer in Berlin durôÙing the sumôÙmer of 1989, right there in the vicinôÙiôÙty of the wall, depicts a city where events seem to be frozen. Though the built enviôÙronôÙment isnãt withôÙout touchôÙes of fadôÙed grandeur here and there (and as many West BerlinôÙers were soon to disôÙcovôÙer, the real urban stateôÙliôÙness was over East), the overôÙall impresôÙsion givôÙen by what was then the red hot cenôÙter of Cold War geopolôÙiôÙtics is that of a dullsville.
The most outôÙwardôÙly interôÙestôÙing feaôÙture in these parts of Berlin at the very end of the nineôÙteen-eightôÙies is, of course, the wall itself: the brutishôÙness of its form, the humôÙdrum menôÙace of its guards, the accuôÙmuôÙlaôÙtion of grafôÙfiôÙti both politôÙiôÙcal and apoôÙlitôÙiôÙcal. At one point, the touristãs camôÙcorder capôÙtures the memoôÙriôÙals for fallôÙen wall jumpers, the most recent of which, a cerôÙtain Chris GuefôÙfroy, had made his fateôÙful escape attempt from the East that past FebôÙruôÙary. HisôÙtoôÙry would soon immorôÙtalôÙize him as the last perôÙson to be shot tryôÙing to get over the wall, though not the last to die doing so. That title belongs to WinôÙfried FreudenôÙberg, who in March of 1989 fell from a balôÙloon heãd rigged up to fly across the borôÙder. At this point, when the rapid urban evoôÙluôÙtion of the reuniôÙfied GerôÙman capôÙiôÙtal has long since made it one of the most popôÙuôÙlar cities in Europe, neiôÙther she nor GuefôÙfroy would recôÙogôÙnize the forôÙmer East Berlin they were desôÙperôÙate to escape ã nor, for that matôÙter, the West Berlin of which they dreamed.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
How the Berlin Wall Worked: The EngiôÙneerôÙing & StrucôÙturôÙal Design of the Wall That ForôÙmiôÙdaôÙbly DividôÙed East & West
See Berlin Before and After World War II in StarôÙtling ColôÙor Video
The GoldôÙen Age of Berlin Comes to Life in the ClasôÙsic, Avant-Garde Film, Berlin: SymôÙphoôÙny of a MetropôÙoôÙlis (1927)
The Dos & Donãts of DriôÙving to West Berlin DurôÙing the Cold War: A Weird Piece of Ephemera from the 1980s
Bruce SpringôÙsteen Plays East Berlin in 1988: Iãm Not Here For Any GovôÙernôÙment. Iãve Come to Play Rock
Watch Samuel BeckôÙett Walk the Streets of Berlin Like a Boss, 1969
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: M.I.T. Computer Program Predicts in 1973 That Civilization Will End by 2040
description: In 1704, Isaac NewôÙton preôÙdictôÙed the end of the world someôÙtime around (or after, ãbut not beforeã) the year 2060, using a strange series o
body:
In 1704, Isaac NewôÙton preôÙdictôÙed the end of the world someôÙtime around (or after, ãbut not beforeã) the year 2060, using a strange series of mathôÙeôÙmatôÙiôÙcal calôÙcuôÙlaôÙtions. Rather than study what he called the ãbook of nature,ã he took as his source the supôÙposed propheôÙcies of the Book of RevôÙeôÙlaôÙtion. While such preôÙdicôÙtions have always been cenôÙtral to ChrisôÙtianôÙiôÙty, it is starôÙtling for modôÙern peoôÙple to look back and see the famed astronomer and physiôÙcist indulging them. For NewôÙton, howôÙevôÙer, as Matthew StanôÙley writes at SciôÙence, ãlayôÙing the founôÙdaôÙtion of modôÙern physics and astronôÙoôÙmy was a bit of a sideshow. He believed that his truôÙly imporôÙtant work was deciôÙpherôÙing ancient scripôÙtures and uncovôÙerôÙing the nature of the ChrisôÙtôÙian reliôÙgion.ã
Over three hunôÙdred years latôÙer, we still have plenôÙty of reliôÙgious doomôÙsayôÙers preôÙdictôÙing the end of the world with Bible codes. But in recent times, their ranks have seemôÙingôÙly been joined by sciôÙenôÙtists whose only proôÙfessed aim is interôÙpretôÙing data from cliôÙmate research and susôÙtainôÙabilôÙiôÙty estiôÙmates givôÙen popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion growth and dwinôÙdling resources. The sciôÙenôÙtifôÙic preôÙdicôÙtions do not draw on ancient texts or theôÙolôÙoôÙgy, nor involve final batôÙtles between good and evil. Though there may be plagues and othôÙer horôÙriôÙble reckôÙonôÙings, these are preôÙdictably causal outôÙcomes of over-proôÙducôÙtion and conôÙsumpôÙtion rather than divine wrath. Yet by some strange fluke, the sciôÙence has arrived at the same apocôÙaôÙlypôÙtic date as NewôÙton, plus or minus a decade or two.
The ãend of the worldã in these sceôÙnarôÙios means the end of modôÙern life as we know it: the colôÙlapse of indusôÙtriôÙalôÙized sociôÙeties, large-scale agriôÙculôÙturôÙal proôÙducôÙtion, supôÙply chains, staôÙble cliôÙmates, nation statesãÎ. Since the late sixôÙties, an elite sociôÙety of wealthy indusôÙtriôÙalôÙists and sciôÙenôÙtists known as the Club of Rome (a freôÙquent playôÙer in many conôÙspirôÙaôÙcy theôÙoôÙries) has foreôÙseen these disôÙasôÙters in the earôÙly 21st cenôÙtuôÙry. One of the sources of their vision is a comôÙputôÙer proôÙgram develôÙoped at MIT by comôÙputôÙing pioôÙneer and sysôÙtems theôÙoôÙrist Jay ForôÙrester, whose modôÙel of globôÙal susôÙtainôÙabilôÙiôÙty, one of the first of its kind, preôÙdictôÙed civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtionôÙal colôÙlapse in 2040. ãWhat the comôÙputôÙer enviôÙsioned in the 1970s has by and large been comôÙing true,ã claims Paul RatôÙner at Big Think.
Those preôÙdicôÙtions include popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion growth and polôÙluôÙtion levôÙels, ãworsôÙenôÙing qualôÙiôÙty of life,ã and ãdwinôÙdling natôÙurôÙal resources.ã In the video at the top, see AusôÙtraliôÙaãs ABC explain the computerãs calôÙcuôÙlaôÙtions, ãan elecôÙtronôÙic guidôÙed tour of our globôÙal behavôÙior since 1900, and where that behavôÙior will lead us,ã says the preôÙsenôÙter. The graph spans the years 1900 to 2060. ãQualôÙiôÙty of lifeã begins to sharply decline after 1940, and by 2020, the modôÙel preôÙdicts, the metôÙric conôÙtracts to turn-of-the-cenôÙtuôÙry levôÙels, meetôÙing the sharp increase of the ãZed Curveã that charts polôÙluôÙtion levôÙels. (ABC revisôÙitôÙed this reportôÙing in 1999 with Club of Rome memôÙber KeiôÙth Suter.)
You can probôÙaôÙbly guess the restãor you can read all about it in the 1972 Club of Rome-pubôÙlished report LimôÙits to Growth, which drew wide popôÙuôÙlar attenôÙtion to Jay Forresterãs books Urban DynamôÙics (1969) and World DynamôÙics (1971). ForôÙrester, a figôÙure of NewôÙtonôÙian stature in the worlds of comôÙputôÙer sciôÙence and manôÙageôÙment and sysôÙtems theoryãthough not, like NewôÙton, a BibôÙliôÙcal propheôÙcy enthusiastãmore or less endorsed his conôÙcluôÙsions to the end of his life in 2016. In one of his last interôÙviews, at the age of 98, he told the MIT TechôÙnolôÙoôÙgy Review, ãI think the books stand all right.ã But he also cauôÙtioned against actôÙing withôÙout sysôÙtemôÙatôÙic thinkôÙing in the face of the globôÙalôÙly interôÙreôÙlatôÙed issues the Club of Rome omiôÙnousôÙly calls ãthe probôÙlemôÙatôÙicã:
Time after time ãÎ youãll find peoôÙple are reactôÙing to a probôÙlem, they think they know what to do, and they donãt realôÙize that what theyãre doing is makôÙing a probôÙlem. This is a vicious [cycle], because as things get worse, there is more incenôÙtive to do things, and it gets worse and worse.
Where this vague warnôÙing is supôÙposed to leave us is uncerôÙtain. If the curôÙrent course is dire, ãunsysôÙtemôÙatôÙicã soluôÙtions may be worse? This theôÙoôÙry also seems to leave powôÙerôÙfulôÙly vestôÙed human agents (like Exxonãs execôÙuôÙtives) wholôÙly unacôÙcountôÙable for the comôÙing colôÙlapse. LimôÙits to Growthãscoffed at and disôÙparagôÙingôÙly called ãneo-MalthuôÙsianã by a host of libôÙerôÙtarôÙiôÙan critôÙicsãstands on far surôÙer eviôÙdenôÙtiary footôÙing than Newtonãs weird preôÙdicôÙtions, and its cliôÙmate foreôÙcasts, notes ChrisôÙtôÙian ParôÙenôÙti, ãwere alarmôÙingôÙly preôÙscient.ã But for all this doom and gloom itãs worth bearôÙing in mind that modôÙels of the future are not, in fact, the future. There are hard times ahead, but no theôÙoôÙry, no matôÙter how sophisôÙtiôÙcatôÙed, can account for every variôÙable.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2018.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
In 1953, a TeleôÙphone-ComôÙpaôÙny ExecôÙuôÙtive PreôÙdicts the Rise of ModôÙern SmartôÙphones and Video Calls
In 1922, a NovôÙelôÙist PreôÙdicts What the World Will Look Like in 2022: WireôÙless TeleôÙphones, 8ãHour Flights to Europe & More
In 1704, Isaac NewôÙton PreôÙdicts the World Will End in 2060
Itãs the End of the World as We Know It: The ApocôÙaôÙlypse Gets VisuôÙalôÙized in an InvenôÙtive Map from 1486
Watch the DestrucôÙtion of PomôÙpeii by Mount VesuôÙvius, Re-CreôÙatôÙed with ComôÙputôÙer AniôÙmaôÙtion (79 AD)
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: Hear Robert Johnsonãs ãCome On in My Kitchenã in Remarkably Restored Audio, Taken from a Rare Test Pressing
description:
in Music |   	May 26th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Robert JohnôÙson died at just 27 years old, some say as a conôÙseôÙquence of sellôÙing his soul
body:
in Music |   	May 26th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Robert JohnôÙson died at just 27 years old, some say as a conôÙseôÙquence of sellôÙing his soul to the devôÙil at a crossôÙroads. But before his time came, he manôÙaged to record 29 songs, a scant body of work that nevôÙerôÙtheôÙless secured his artisôÙtic immorôÙtalôÙiôÙty as one of the most influôÙenôÙtial blues musiôÙcians of all time. Itãs unforôÙtuôÙnate that his recordôÙings, all of them made between 1936 and 1937 in less-than-ideôÙal stuôÙdio conôÙdiôÙtions even for the time, leave someôÙthing to be desired in the audio qualôÙiôÙty departôÙment. But now, some 90 years latôÙer, sound restorôÙer Nick DelôÙlow has been uploadôÙing relôÙaôÙtiveôÙly crisp digôÙiôÙtized ãtest pressôÙingsã of JohnôÙsonãs songs to YouTube: last month, for examôÙple, we feaôÙtured one of ãCross Road Bluesã here on Open CulôÙture.
In the video above, youãll find a simôÙiôÙlarôÙly highôÙer-qualôÙiôÙty verôÙsion of ãCome On in My Kitchen,ã a song acknowlôÙedged as an earôÙly demonôÙstraôÙtion of the young JohnôÙsonãs othôÙerôÙworldôÙly musiôÙcal powôÙer. You may notice that the title labels this parôÙticôÙuôÙlar recordôÙing as ãtake one.ã JohnôÙson also recordôÙed a much difôÙferôÙent secôÙond take, which his label Vocalion Records released in 1937, posôÙsiôÙbly because it soundôÙed less mournôÙful and thus ã accordôÙing to record-indusôÙtry logôÙic ã more viable as a hit.
Though take one now seems to be regardôÙed as the ãtrueã renôÙdiôÙtion of the song by his seriôÙous enthuôÙsiôÙasts, the pubôÙlic didôÙnãt get to hear it until 1961, when it was includôÙed on the comôÙpiôÙlaôÙtion King of the Delta Blues Singers that did more than any othôÙer release to win JohnôÙson his posthuôÙmous fan base.
It is, admitôÙtedôÙly, not easy to imagôÙine the first take of ãCome On in My Kitchenã sweepôÙing the dance halls, even with this sound qualôÙiôÙty much improved from the verôÙsion on King of the Delta Blues Singers. But the reaôÙsons JohnôÙsonãs music has endured so long have less to do with his abilôÙiôÙty to get a crowd movôÙing than with his comôÙbiôÙnaôÙtion of underôÙstatôÙed virôÙtuôÙosôÙiôÙty and preterôÙnatôÙurôÙal-soundôÙing abilôÙiôÙty to reach into genôÙuineôÙly hauntôÙing emoôÙtionôÙal realms. Like many canonôÙiôÙcal singer-songôÙwritôÙers who died young, he seems always to be and remain someôÙhow oldôÙer than us, his lisôÙtenôÙers, even as we reach (and indeed pass) midôÙdle age. OccaôÙsionôÙalôÙly, the release of nevôÙer-before-heard recordôÙings or pressôÙings reveals the true edge of immaôÙtuôÙriôÙty in such figôÙures; with JohnôÙson, it only deepôÙens his legôÙend.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
A NewôÙly DisôÙcovôÙered RecordôÙing Lets You Hear Delta Blues LegôÙend Robert JohnôÙson in StunôÙning ClarôÙiôÙty
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: The Myth of Sisyphus Wonderfully Animated in an Oscar-Nominated Short Film (1974)
description:
in Film |   	May 26th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even if you donãt know the myth by name, you know the stoôÙry. In Greek mytholôÙoôÙgy,ô SisyôÙphus
body:
in Film |   	May 26th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even if you donãt know the myth by name, you know the stoôÙry. In Greek mytholôÙoôÙgy,ô SisyôÙphus, King of Corinth, was punôÙished ãfor his self-aggranôÙdizôÙing craftiôÙness and deceitôÙfulôÙness by being forced to roll an immense boulôÙder up a hill, only to watch it roll back down, repeatôÙing this action for eterôÙniôÙty.ãô In modôÙern times, thisô stoôÙry inspired Albert Camus to write ãThe Myth of SisyôÙphus,ã an essay where he famousôÙly introôÙduced his conôÙcept of the ãabsurdã and idenôÙtiôÙfied SisyôÙphus as the absurd hero. And it proôÙvidôÙed the creôÙative mateôÙrôÙiôÙal for a breathôÙtakôÙingôÙly good aniôÙmaôÙtion creôÙatôÙedô by MarôÙcell Jankovics in 1974. The film, notes the annoôÙtaôÙtion that accomôÙpaôÙnies the aniôÙmaôÙtion on YouTube, is ãpreôÙsentôÙed in a sinôÙgle, unbroôÙken shot, conôÙsistôÙing of a dynamôÙic line drawôÙing of SisyôÙphus, the stone, and the mounôÙtainôÙside.ã FitôÙtingôÙly, Jankovicsã litôÙtle masôÙterôÙpiece was nomôÙiôÙnatôÙed for the Best AniôÙmatôÙed Short Film at the 48th AcadôÙeôÙmy Awards.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2015.
If you would like to supôÙport the misôÙsion of Open CulôÙture, conôÙsidôÙer makôÙingô a donaôÙtion to our site. Itãs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions will help us conôÙtinôÙue proôÙvidôÙing the best free culôÙturôÙal and eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal mateôÙriôÙals to learnôÙers everyôÙwhere. You can conôÙtribute through PayôÙPal, PatreôÙon, and VenôÙmo (@openculture). Thanks!
RelatôÙed ConôÙtentô 
Mythos: An AniôÙmaôÙtion Retells TimeôÙless Greek Myths with Abstract ModôÙern Designs

title: Eight London Underground Restrooms Get Vibrant Refurbs with Illustrated Tiles
description: Home to some of Londonãs most iconic landmarks, the City of Westminster sees around 25 million of tourists every year. Add that number to resident
body:
Home to some of Londonãs most iconic landmarks, the City of Westminster sees around 25 million of tourists every year. Add that number to residents and professionals who transit through central London daily, and weãre talking lots of people using the Underground, a.k.a. the Tube. Fortunately for travelers, eight stations are getting restroom upgrades with a big assist from Hugh Broughton Architects.
When the Victorians established the first underground train network in the mid-to-late 1800s, they incorporated arched details and tile work that is preserved today, often augmented with contemporary nods to the cityãs history and culture. Opened in 1863 along the Metropolitan Line, Baker Street is one of the oldest stations. In a design convention seen throughout the Underground, decorated tiles added later feature a silhouette of Sherlock Holmesãa tribute to the characterãs fictional address on the stationãs titular street.

Tile designs are richly embedded throughout the system. An architect named Leslie Green is known for a slew of deep red tile facades on more than a dozen central stations. Some interiors are covered practically top to bottom with the material, with the location names and ãWay Outã signs made entirely from ceramic. Bethnal Green station features fantastic relief tiles of London icons, and a few years ago, a design student named Jeffrey Miller even repurposed the Tubeãs own waste into Art Nouveau-style tiles modeled on historic versions.
Hugh Broughton Architectsã redesign continues this tradition, but it also serves the practical purpose of making these spaces safer, more inclusive, and more accessible. ãThere is a clear demand for public toilets across London, and many councils struggle with the costs surrounding maintenance and issues around antisocial behaviour,ã says a statement. ãTo address this issue, the council is creating a series of inclusive facilities that demonstrate high-quality design and enshrine as much civic pride as the Victorians displayed when they first started looking at a proper sanitation system for the general public in the 19th century.ã
The facilities at Victoria Embankment, Parliament Street, Piccadilly Circus, Green Park, Carnaby Street, Westminster Pier, Covent Garden, and Leicester Square are all getting vibrant makeovers. The tiles incorporate designs by artist and illustrator James Lambert, who interprets the distinctive character of each location through historical motifs and iconography. Among numerous playful references, youãll find patterns consisting of the Kingãs Guards in their iconically tall, black, furry helmets and a tribute to George John Vulliamyãs giant sphinxes that guard Cleopatraãs Needle on the Embankment.
The restrooms are being refurbished in succession. Victoria Street opened in February 2025, followed by Parliament Street, and now Piccadilly Circus and Green Park. The next will be Carnaby Street and Westminster Bridge. Follow updates on the firmãs Instagram.

schema.headline: Eight London Underground Restrooms Get Vibrant Refurbs with Illustrated Tiles

title: In a Single Year, Ocean Census Scientists Discovered More than 1,100 New Marine Species
description: Oceans cover more than 70 percent of the earth, and yet there is so much we have yet to understand about these vast and biodiverse expanses. Even
body:
Oceans cover more than 70 percent of the earth, and yet there is so much we have yet to understand about these vast and biodiverse expanses. Even though weãve recorded tens of thousands of marine creatures over the decades and scientists are discovering new species all the time, weãve really only scratched the surface. According to The Nippon Foundation-Nekton Ocean Census, up to 90 percent of ocean life remains a mystery.
A major collaborative program involving Schmidt Ocean Institute and many others, Ocean Census is accelerating the pace at which marine life is found and documented. Between April 1, 2025, and March 31, 2026, alone, the initiativeãs researchers officially discovered 1,121 new species through 13 expeditions and nine ãspecies discovery workshopsã involving leading scientists around the globe.
Carnivorous ãDeath Ballã Sponge (Chondrocladia sp.), Phylum: Porifera. Taxonomist: Dr. Javier Francisco Cristobo RodrûÙguez. Image courtesy of ROV SuBastian/Schmidt Ocean Institute
ãWith many species at risk of disappearing before they are even documented, we are in a race against time to understand and protect ocean life,ã says Dr. Michelle Taylor, who is Head of Science for Ocean Census. ãToo many species remain in limbo for years because the process of formally describing them is too slow. We urgently need to change thatãÎEvery new speciesãwhether a shark or a spongeãdeepens our understanding of marine ecosystems and the benefits they provide for the planet.ã
Explore hundreds more newly discovered species on the Ocean Census website.
Sea cave shrimp (Caridion sp.1), Phylum: Arthropoda. Taxonomist: Dr. Hossein Ashrafi. Image courtesy of The Nippon Foundation-Nekton Ocean Census/Hossein Ashrafi
Ribbon worm (Drepanophoridae sp.), Phylum: Nemertea. Taxonomist: Dr. Svetlana Maslakova. Image courtesy of The Nippon Foundation-Nekton Ocean Census/Gustav Paulay
Dwarfgoby (Eviota sp.), Phylum: Chordata. Taxonomist: Dr. Chris Goatley. Image courtesy of Chris Goatley/The Nippon Foundation-Nekton Ocean Census
Mystery Ridge Sea Pen (Ptilella sp. OCSS_1146), Phylum: Cnidaria. Taxonomist: Dr. Raissa Hogan. Image courtesy of Paul Satchell/The Nippon Foundation-Nekton Ocean Census/Schmidt Ocean Institute
ãGlass castleã worm (Dalhousiella yabukii), Phylum: Annelida. Taxonomist: Dr. Naoto Jimi. Image courtesy of The Nippon Foundation-Nekton Ocean Census/JAMSTEC
Nicella sp.1. Phylum: Cnidaria. Image courtesy of The Nippon Foundation-Nekton Ocean Census
Burrowing Sea Anemone (Harenactis sp.), Phylum: Cnidaria. Taxonomist: Dr. AgustûÙn Garese. Image courtesy of The Nippon Foundation-Nekton Ocean Census/AgustûÙn Garese

title: Ava Roth Collaborates with Insects to Create 'Kintsu-Bee' Ceramic Vessels
description: In Ava Rothãs sculpture practice, a finished piece is the result of careful planning and tending, but the outcome can only be predicted so much. W
body:
In Ava Rothãs sculpture practice, a finished piece is the result of careful planning and tending, but the outcome can only be predicted so much. Whether creating wooden frameworks or organic embroideries, the artist leaves it to bees to create the ultimate form.
Roth has long invited the honeycomb-building insects to play a role in her work, often adding wonderfully bulbous constructions that occasionally disrupt the artistãs carefully placed boundaries. Wooden pieces are mandala-like and take on the quality of low reliefs once the bees have done their part. Recently, she leapt into the three-dimensional realm via ceramics and a time-honored tradition of repair in her series Kintsu-Bee.

The new body of work is a play on the Japanese word kintsugi, which describes a traditional method of repairing ceramics with metallic lacquer. The process embraces the nature of the breakage itself, mending the vessel yet highlighting the cracks as a way of embracing the objectãs history rather than trying to camouflage it. In Rothãs iteration, bees are invited to reconstruct the missing parts, guided around forms to create the missing handle of a mug or fill in the fissures of a dinner plate.
ãMirroring the philosophy of kintsugi, the unique architecture of the comb acts both as a restorative measure and as a visual memory of the past,ã says a statement.ô ãWhen extracted, the delicacy and complexity of the composite objectsãhalf human and half insectãtell a story not just of human violence but of the earthãs capacity for repair.ã
See more on Rothãs Instagram.

schema.headline: Ava Roth Collaborates with Insects to Create 'Kintsu-Bee' Ceramic Vessels
